description: Comprehensive guide to two-dimensional arrays: declaration, initialization, accessing elements, row-major order, passing to functions, dynamic 2D arrays, and practical examples.

sections.title: What is a 2D Array?
sections.definition: A 2D array is an array of arrays. It represents a matrix or table with rows and columns. Elements are accessed using two indices: arr[row][col].
sections.analogy: A chessboard: rows 0-7, columns 0-7. Each cell is an element.

sections.title: Declaring 2D Arrays
sections.definition: Syntax: type arrayName[rows][cols]; rows and cols must be compile-time constants for static arrays.

sections.title: Accessing 2D Array Elements
sections.definition: Use row index and column index. Both start at 0. Valid indices: row 0 to rows-1, col 0 to cols-1.

sections.title: Memory Layout (Row-Major Order)
sections.definition: C++ stores 2D arrays in row-major order: all elements of row 0 first, then row 1, etc. Contiguous memory.

sections.title: Passing 2D Arrays to Functions
sections.definition: When passing 2D array, second dimension must be specified (or use pointer to array).

sections.title: Dynamic 2D Arrays
sections.definition: Several ways: array of pointers (each row separately) or single flat array with manual indexing.

sections.title: Practical Examples: Matrix Operations
sections.definition: Common operations: addition, multiplication, transpose, etc.

sections.title: 2D Arrays of Characters (Strings Array)
sections.definition: 2D char arrays can store multiple strings. Each row is a C-style string.
